era_context: The early Classical period of ancient Greece, during the reign of Darius I in the Achaemenid Persian Empire. Athens is consolidating democratic reforms under Cleisthenes (implemented ~508 BCE); Sparta dominates the Peloponnese. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is in its final decades; in India, the Buddha is in his final years or recently deceased (dating disputed). The Mediterranean world is experiencing increased trade and colonisation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman awareness
Mesoamerica (no written records contemporary with this year)

events.title: Probable death of Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha)
events.region: Magadha, Indian subcontinent (modern Bihar/Nepal border region)
events.description: Siddhartha Gautama, founder of Buddhism, is traditionally dated to have died around this year, though scholarly consensus places his death anywhere from 544 to 400 BCE. Buddhist texts record his death (parinirvana) at Kushinara in the Malla kingdom. His death marked the transition from his direct teaching to the establishment of the monastic sangha as the primary vehicle for Buddhist transmission. The exact year remains one of the most contested dates in ancient history.
events.certainty_note: Buddhist tradition places the Buddha's death in this year or nearby, but modern scholarship dates his life anywhere from 566–486 BCE to 480–400 BCE. No contemporary written records exist. The dating depends on retroactive calculation from later texts and is highly uncertain.

events.title: Athenian ostracism of Themistocles (or shortly after)
events.description: Themistocles, the Athenian statesman and military strategist who would later lead the Greek naval victory at Salamis (480 BCE), may have been ostracised around this time, though the exact year is debated. Ostracism was a democratic procedure allowing citizens to vote to exile a prominent figure for ten years. Themistocles' ostracism is variously dated to 471, 470, or the 490s BCE. If dated to 495, it would reflect factional tensions in Athens before the Persian Wars.
events.certainty_note: The ostracism of Themistocles is historically confirmed, but its precise date is uncertain. Ancient sources give conflicting dates. Archaeological ostraca confirm the practice but do not provide precise dating. Assigned to 495 by some scholars but more commonly placed in the 470s BCE.

events.title: Ionian Revolt against Persian rule (ongoing)
events.region: Ionia (western coast of Asia Minor), Persian Empire
events.description: The Ionian Revolt (c. 499–493 BCE) against Achaemenid Persian rule was in its early stages around 495 BCE. Greek city-states in Ionia, led by Miletus, rebelled against Persian taxation and governance. The revolt was supported by Athens and Eretria but ultimately suppressed by Persian forces under Darius I. The destruction of Miletus in 494 BCE was a major consequence. This conflict set the stage for the later Greco-Persian Wars.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Miletus and other Ionian sites

events.certainty_note: The Ionian Revolt is well-attested in Herodotus and confirmed by archaeological evidence of destruction layers. The dating of 499–493 BCE is scholarly consensus, with 495 falling squarely within the revolt period.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Buddha's death is highly contested and represents one of the most significant chronological disputes in ancient history. Some scholars place his death as early as 544 BCE or as late as 400 BCE. The traditional date of 495 BCE is one of several competing chronologies (the 'long chronology' vs. 'short chronology' debate). No contemporary evidence exists to confirm any specific year. Themistocles' ostracism is also disputed in date; many modern scholars place it in the 470s BCE rather than the 490s, which would remove it from this year entirely.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 495 BCE is heavily dependent on Greek sources, particularly Herodotus, who wrote ~50 years after these events. For Indian history, the record is even more problematic: Buddhist texts were compiled centuries after the Buddha's death and contain legendary elements. Chinese sources for this period (Spring and Autumn period) are more reliable but focus on East Asian events. The record is biased toward Greek and Mediterranean history; events in Persia, India, and China are either poorly documented or known only through later compilations. Archaeological evidence (destruction layers, ostraca, inscriptions) provides some grounding but cannot always be precisely dated to a single year.

graph_edges.note: Buddha's death marks the beginning of the post-parinirvana Buddhist tradition
Ionian Revolt led to the destruction of Miletus in 494 BCE
Ionian Revolt contributed to tensions leading to the First Persian invasion of Greece
